coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel incorporating Bi2O3/BiOCOOH for effective adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of lignin

coredata.dc:description: A novel photocatalytic adsorbent, a cellulose nanofibrils based hydrogel incorporating carbon dots and Bi2O3/BiOCOOH (designated as CCHBi), was developed to address lignin pollution. CCHBi exhibited an adsorption capacity of 435.0 mg/g, 8.9 times greater than that of commercial activated carbon. This enhanced adsorption performance was attributed to the 3D porous structure constructed using cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), which increased the specific surface area and provided additional sorption sites. Adsorption and photocatalytic experiments showed that CCHBi had a photocatalytic degradation rate constant of 0.0140 min−1, 3.1 times higher than that of Bi2O3/BiOCOOH. The superior photocatalytic performance of CCHBi was due to the Z-scheme photocatalytic system constructed by carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils and Bi2O3/BiOCOOH, which facilitated the separation of photoinduced charge carriers. Additionally, the stability of CCHBi was confirmed through consecutive cycles of adsorption and photocatalysis, maintaining a removal efficiency of 85 % after ten cycles. The enhanced stability was due to the 3D porous structure constructed by CNs, which safeguarded the Bi2O3/BiOCOOH. This study validates the potential of CCHBi for high-performance lignin removal and promotes the application of CNs in developing new photocatalytic adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils hydrogel incorporating Bi2O3/BiOCOOH for effective adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of lignin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel photocatalytic adsorbent, a cellulose nanofibrils based hydrogel incorporating carbon dots and Bi2O3/BiOCOOH (designated as CCHBi), was developed to address lignin pollution. CCHBi exhibited an adsorption capacity of 435.0 mg/g, 8.9 times greater than that of commercial activated carbon. This enhanced adsorption performance was attributed to the 3D porous structure constructed using cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), which increased the specific surface area and provided additional sorption sites. Adsorption and photocatalytic experiments showed that CCHBi had a photocatalytic degradation rate constant of 0.0140 min−1, 3.1 times higher than that of Bi2O3/BiOCOOH. The superior photocatalytic performance of CCHBi was due to the Z-scheme photocatalytic system constructed by carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils and Bi2O3/BiOCOOH, which facilitated the separation of photoinduced charge carriers. Additionally, the stability of CCHBi was confirmed through consecutive cycles of adsorption and photocatalysis, maintaining a removal efficiency of 85 % after ten cycles. The enhanced stability was due to the 3D porous structure constructed by CNs, which safeguarded the Bi2O3/BiOCOOH. This study validates the potential of CCHBi for high-performance lignin removal and promotes the application of CNs in developing new photocatalytic adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin is a complex organic polymer found widely in plant cell walls (Kai et al., 2016). Industries such as pulp and paper, biorefineries, and agriculture release effluents rich in lignin (Haq et al., 2020; Shamaei et al., 2020), causing environmental pollution and negatively affecting water quality and ecosystem health. Thus, addressing lignin pollution is crucial for environmental conservation and sustainable development.

To address the challenges posed by lignin-rich effluents, various advanced oxidation techniques have been proposed, including ozone treatment (Shi et al., 2020), ultraviolet (UV) irradiation (Wang et al., 2016), Fenton processes (Kent et al., 2015), and photocatalytic methods (Wang et al., 2019). Photocatalytic technology, which harnesses solar energy, exhibits strong oxidation capabilities. Its application in lignin removal through photocatalytic degradation is environmentally friendly and highly promising. Bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) as a photocatalyst has gained significant attention. The composite of Bi2O3 and bismuth oxycarbonate (BiOCOOH) has shown remarkable efficacy in photocatalytic treatment of lignin-containing wastewater. Zhang et al. (2022) synthesized Bi2O3/BiOCOOH powder (BiP) through calcination, using BiOCOOH as a sacrificial precursor, and this BiP served as an efficient photocatalyst.

Zhou, Xie, Su, et al. (2023) synthesized BiP for efficient photocatalytic lignin degradation. Their findings showed a 90 % lignin degradation rate under visible light exposure for 200 min. However, challenges in recovering BiP limit its practical application. A promising solution involves incorporating BiP on a hydrogel. This method not only addresses the issue of recovering nanopowder photocatalysts but also prevents the aggregation of photocatalyst particles, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), nanoscale fibers derived from plant cell walls (Zimmermann et al., 2010), possess a large specific surface area and exceptional mechanical properties (Shi et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2020). CNs-based hydrogels offer numerous advantages, including optimized geometrical size, mechanical properties, cycling performance, and adsorption capacity. Furthermore, CNs-based hydrogels provide efficient mass transfer channels, making them effective carriers for nanoparticles. Consequently, CNs-based hydrogels are promising candidates for loading BiP.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with unique optical properties (Li et al., 2022; Wareing et al., 2021). CDs anchored to various supports, such as polymers, mesoporous materials, and metal-organic frameworks, have been reported to be efficient in adsorbing environmental pollutants. Moreover, CDs exhibit remarkable electronic properties, functioning as proficient electron donors and acceptors (Shah & Lin, 2017). Their exceptional electron transfer capabilities integrate seamlessly into various photocatalytic systems, significantly enhancing overall activity. For instance, Tian et al. (2023) developed a CDs-modified g-C3N4/BiVO4, noting a significant improvement in charge separation. Yan et al. (2023) used a hydrothermal approach to immobilize CDs onto Bi2WO6, observing that the presence of CDs significantly reduced the electron-hole recombination rate. However, the challenge of separating and recovering CDs limits their widespread application. A promising solution involves immobilizing CDs on hydrogels, such as CNs-based hydrogels.
The details of the chemical reagents used are provided in the Supplementary Material.

Bleached wood pulp (BWP, 5.0 g) was thoroughly mixed with water (300 mL). 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy free radical (TEMPO, 0.078 g) and NaBr (0.515 g) were dissolved in 200 mL of water to prepare Solution A. Solution A was then slowly added to the BWP suspension. Subsequently, 30 mmol of NaClO was added to initiate the reaction. The pH of the suspension was maintained between 10 and 11 throughout the reaction. After five hours, 5 mL of acetone was added to terminate the reaction. The oxidation suspension was dialyzed against water for five days and homogenized five times to fabricate the target product, CNs. The CNs had a carboxyl group content of 1.2 mmol/g and showed an average width of 24 nm and length of 1036 nm, resulting in an aspect ratio of 43.

Citric acid (4.2 g) was placed in a 150 mL Teflon-lined high-pressure autoclave reactor. Ethylenediamine (5.36 mL) and 100 mL of water were then added. After shaking for 30 min, the reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to 25 °C, the reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane. The liquid was then transferred to a dialysis bag and dialyzed for four days to obtain a CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils (CDs@CNs)
ce:section.ce:para: A CNs suspension (10 g, 0.1 wt%) was mixed with borate buffer (10 g, 0.01 M, pH 9) using sonication. Next, N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.04 g), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.05 g), and CDs (1.0 g, 0.5 wt%) were sequentially dispersed. The resulting suspension was then stirred at 25 °C for one day. The sample, designated as CDs@CNs, was subsequently dialyzed against water for four days.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (0.97 g) and glycol (1 mL) were dissolved in ethanol (60 mL). The solution was placed in a 150 mL Teflon-lined high-pressure autoclave reactor. The reaction was then conducted at 160 °C for 12 h (Zhou, Xie, Su, et al., 2023). After cooling to 25 °C, the precipitate was washed with water and ethanol by centrifugation, followed by vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the target product, BiP.

An L16 orthogonal array (OA) experimental design was used to investigate five factors, each with four levels (details in Table S1). This array assessed the impact of these factors on lignin removal rate. The optimal factor combination was identified as A3B3C1D2E4 (see Table S2). The procedure involved adding acrylic acid (AA, 2.0 g), sodium hydroxide (5 M, 4.72 mL), and a CDs@CNs suspension (2.0 g) to a round-bottom flask. BiP (0.02 g) was then evenly dispersed, followed by the addition of N, N′-Methylenebis(2-propenamide) (MBA, 0.02 g). Potassium persulfate (PPS, 0.02 g) was then added to initiate the reaction, which was kept in the oven at 65 °C for 60 min. The resulting sample, CCHBi-3, was thoroughly washed with water three times to remove unreacted reagents; its chemical structure is illustrated in Fig. S1. Samples with varying weights of BiP (0.005, 0.01, 0.02, and 0.03 g) were labeled as CCHBi-1, CCHBi-2, CCHBi-3, and CCHBi-4. Additionally, a control hydrogel, CHBi-3, was fabricated by omitting CDs.

For batch adsorption studies, 80 mg of adsorbents were added to 200 mL of sodium ligninsulfonate solutions in 250 mL round-bottom flasks. The flasks were shaken at 300 rpm. Unless otherwise specified, C
0 , t, pH, and T were set at 100 mg/L, 90 min, 3, and 25 °C, respectively. Isothermal adsorption tests covered C
0 ranging from 50 to 6000 mg/L, while kinetic studies varied t from 0 to 90 min. The effects of pH were evaluated across a range of 3 to 11, and the effects of T were examined from 25 °C to 65 °C. After adsorption, the adsorbents were separated from the lignin solution. Residual lignin concentration was determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ = 280 nm.

An 80 mg sample was dispersed in 200mL of sodium ligninsulfonate solution. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 90min and then exposed to UV light from a 500 W mercury lamp (Fig. S2). At specific intervals, a fixed volume of the reaction solution was sampled from the photocatalytic device. The corresponding concentration (C
t ) was measured at 280 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The removal rate was calculated using the formula “ R
%
=
C t
/
C 0
”, and the degradation rate constant (k) was determined using the pseudo-first-order model “ Ln
C 0
/
C t
= kt ”.

The micromorphology of the fabricated sample was examined with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). Prior to SEM analysis, the sample was freeze-fractured in liquid nitrogen and coated with gold. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a spectrometer (IS10, Nicorette, USA) in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 to observe the functional groups on the sample surface. The crystalline structures of the samples were analyzed with X-ray diffraction (XRD; D8, AXS, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed with an F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Prismatic Technology Co., Ltd.).

The synthesis of CCHBi involved several key steps outlined in Fig. 1 . First, CNs were synthesized via TEMPO-mediated oxidation, followed by high-pressure homogenization. Simultaneously, CDs were prepared via hydrothermal reaction with ethylenediamine, which introduced numerous amino groups. Next, CNs and CDs were crosslinked to form CDs@CNs. The resulting CDs@CNs were then uniformly blended with BiP, AA, and MBA. Finally, PPS was added to initiate free radical polymerization, synthesizing CCHBi.

The microstructures of CNs, CDs@CNs, BiP, and CCHBi-3 were characterized using SEM. CNs exhibited a needle-like morphology with a smooth surface (Xiao et al., 2015), as shown in Fig. 2a. CDs were nearly spherical, ranging in size from approximately 1.6 nm to 4.0 nm, and dispersed well without aggregation (Fig. S3) (Cao et al., 2022; Han et al., 2018). CDs@CNs displayed a distinctive dense fold structure along the surface axis, attributed to the loaded CDs (Fig. 2b) (You et al., 2016). BiP exhibited a nanometer-scale flower morphology (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d illustrates the 3D porous structure of CCHBi-3. The specific surface areas, calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation, were 6.70, 7.02, 30.04, and 38.34 m2/g for CNs, CDs@CNs, BiP, and CCHBi-3, respectively (Fig. S4). Notably, CCHBi-3 had the largest specific surface area.

Fig. 2e shows the XRD pattern of BiP, showing characteristic diffraction patterns of both BiOCOOH (JCPDS: 35–0939) and Bi2O3 (JCPDS: 27–0052). The XRD pattern of BiP exhibited distinct peaks at 24.3°, 28.8°, and 35.1°, corresponding to the (101), (102), and (103) crystal planes of BiOCOOH (Chen et al., 2018), and at 27.1°, 32.4°, and 46.4°, corresponding to the (111), (200), and (220) crystallographic planes of Bi2O3 (Zhou, Xie, Rao, et al., 2023). Additionally, a composite peak at 55.1° was observed, indicative of both the (212) crystal plane of BiOCOOH and the (311) crystallographic plane of Bi2O3. These findings confirmed the coexistence of BiOCOOH and Bi2O3 in BiP (Zhou, Xie, Su, et al., 2023).

Fig. 2f presents the XRD patterns of four samples. CNs exhibited peaks at 14.9°, 16.2°, and 22.7°, corresponding to the (11′0), (110), and (200) crystal planes (Sun et al., 2021). The XRD pattern of CDs@CNs showed no significant changes compared to CNs, indicating that the loading of CDs did not affect the crystal planes of CNs. BiP displayed seven diffraction peaks at 24.3°, 27.1°, 28.8°, 32.4°, 35.1°, 46.4°, and 55.1°. The XRD pattern of CCHBi-3 included characteristic peaks from both CDs@CNs and BiP, confirming the successful incorporation of these components.

Fig. 2g shows the FTIR spectra of CNs, CDs@CNs, BiP, and CCHBi-3. All four samples exhibited a characteristic band in the range of 3000–3700 cm−1, attributed to OH stretching (Tie et al., 2022). CNs and CDs@CNs exhibited an absorption band at 1054 cm−1, assigned to CO stretching (Wang et al., 2018). CDs@CNs also showed a –CO–NH– vibrational peak at 1649 cm−1 (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2023). BiP displayed bands at 1543 and 1377 cm−1, attributed to –COO− stretching vibration of formate esters, a band at 754 cm−1 for –OH bending vibration, and a band in the 500–700 cm−1 range, assigned to BiO stretching (Liu et al., 2020; Tiamduangtawan et al., 2020). CCHBi-3 exhibited characteristic bands from both CDs@CNs and BiP, confirming the successful incorporation of these components.

Fig. 2h presents the full XPS spectrum of CCHBi-3, confirming the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and bismuth (Bi). This result validated the successful incorporation of CDs@CNs and BiP.

Response surface methodology (RSM) was used to assess the effects of pH, T, and C
0 on the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q
e ) of CCHBi-3. Table S3 presents the independent variables along with their actual and coded values. Seventeen experiments were designed and conducted, with the observed and predicted results summarized in Table S4. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) in Table S5 revealed significant t-values and p-values, confirming the substantial influence of these factors. Furthermore, the interactive effects of the three variables on the Q
e of CCHBi-3 are depicted in 3D response surface plots (Fig. 3a-c).

Fig. 3d illustrates the results of adsorption experiments conducted at various pH levels. Within the pH range of 3 to 11, Q
e gradually decreased as pH increased. The point of zero charge (pzc) of CCHBi-3 was found to be 3.2 (Fig. S5). Below pH 3.2, CCHBi-3 exhibited a positive charge, facilitating favorable adsorption with the negatively charged lignin. Conversely, above pH 3.2, both CCHBi-3 and lignin became negatively charged, resulting in a gradual decline in the adsorption capacity of CCHBi-3 (Mostashari et al., 2013). Therefore, the optimal pH for adsorption was identified as 3.

Fig. 3e illustrates the Q
e of CCHBi-3 at temperatures ranging from 25 °C to 65 °C. The results indicated a decrease in Q
e with increasing T, suggesting an exothermic reaction. Furthermore, Gibbs free energy (ΔG), standard enthalpy (ΔH), and standard entropy (ΔS) were computed using the linear fitting of ln(Q
e /C
0 ) versus 1/T and Van't Hoff equations (Fig. S6). The negative ΔG values at all temperatures suggested that the adsorption processes were thermodynamically spontaneous and feasible (Moharrami & Motamedi, 2020). The negative ΔH value (−44.30 kJ/mol) indicated the exothermic nature of the adsorption process (Melo et al., 2018). To align with practical applications, subsequent experiments were conducted at 25 °C.

Fig. 3f illustrates the impact of C
0 on the Q
e of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3, with Q
e in the order: BiP < CHBi-3 < CCHBi-3. These results indicated the positive effects of CNs and CDs on lignin adsorption. Optical images of the overall morphology of these three samples are provided in Fig. S7. To investigate the adsorption process, the experimental results were fitted to the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Fig. 3h and i). The fitting results are shown in Table S6. By comparing the R2 values, the Langmuir model was found to fit the experimental data well, implying monolayer adsorption of lignin onto CCHBi-3. Moreover, the maximum adsorption capacity (Q
max ) was calculated to be 435.0 ± 9.0 mg/g, which was 2.5 times that of BiP (160.7 ± 5.5 mg/g, see Fig. S8) and 8.9 times greater than that of commercial activated carbon (48.9 ± 1.3 mg/g, see Fig. S9). Additionally, CCHBi-3 exhibited a superior lignin adsorption capacity compared to other photocatalysts listed in Table 1 .

The adsorption characteristics of the prepared samples were investigated at various times, as depicted in Fig. 3g. All three samples exhibited similar trends in lignin adsorption, characterized by an initial rapid uptake followed by a gradual approach to equilibrium. The adsorption kinetics were analyzed using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models. The kinetic fitting results for lignin adsorption on CCHBi-3 are presented in Fig. 3j and k, with the corresponding parameter calculations listed in Table S7. The pseudo-second-order model showed a higher R2 value than the pseudo-first-order model, indicating that lignin adsorption by CCHBi-3 was predominantly governed by chemisorption (Andersson et al., 2011).

Fig. 4a illustrates the lignin removal rates of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 over time during adsorption and photocatalytic degradation. After 180 min, BiP achieved a removal rate of 53.78 %. Incorporating BiP into a CNs-based hydrogel to form CHBi-3 enhanced the lignin removal rate to 85.45 %. Further incorporating CDs into CHBi-3 to form CCHBi-3 significantly increased the lignin removal rate to 93.61 %. The kinetics of lignin photocatalytic degradation were also studied. As shown in Fig. 4b, the rate constant (k) for CCHBi-3 was determined to be 0.0140 min−1, which was 3.1 times higher than that of BiP. These findings confirmed the superior photocatalytic activity of CCHBi-3 compared to BiP and CHBi-3. Additionally, the lignin removal rate of CCHBi-3 was compared with other samples reported in previous studies, as summarized in Table 2 . The results indicated that CCHBi-3 exhibited superior performance in lignin removal, serving as an effective photocatalyst for lignin degradation.

The effects of pH, BiP loading, and C
0 on lignin removal rates are presented in Fig. 4c-h. ANOVA confirmed the significant influence of these factors (Tables S8-S10). The results indicated that the optimal conditions were a pH of 3, a BiP loading of 0.02 g (corresponding to CCHBi-3), and a C
0 of 50 mg/L.

Reusability is a critical indicator, necessitating the study of consecutive cycles of adsorption and photocatalysis. Fig. 5 shows the removal rate of CCHBi-3 over ten cycles. The removal rate remained nearly 85 % of the initial value, indicating minimal loss. The enhanced stability was attributed to the 3D porous structure of CCHBi-3, which effectively protected BiP (Zhou et al., 2018). Additionally, ICP-OES leaching tests on CCHBi-3 revealed no detectable traces of bismuth in the treated solution, demonstrating its superior stability (Zhao et al., 2021). FTIR, XRD, and SEM analyses of CCHBi-3 after ten cycles (depicted in Figs. 5b, c, and S10) indicated an insignificant loss of structural and morphological features. These observations suggested that CCHBi-3 maintained its characteristics during the adsorption and photocatalysis process (Li et al., 2020).

As shown by the FTIR analysis (Fig. 6a), after adsorption, the CCHBi-3 spectrum exhibited a noticeable reduction in the following spectral peaks: the O − H band at 3000–3700 cm−1 (Xu et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2016), the −CO − NH− band at 1649 cm−1, two −COO− bands at 1543 cm−1 and 1377 cm−1, and the BiO band at 500–700 cm−1. Additionally, new bands appeared at 1136 cm−1 and 1044 cm−1, attributed to SO and − SO3 2− groups (Ye et al., 2016), respectively. This indicated the involvement of hydroxyl and amino groups in lignin removal.

Fig. 6b illustrates the XPS survey spectra of CCHBi-3 before and after lignin adsorption, confirming the presence of C, N, O, and Bi elements. The emergence of the S element suggested successful lignin adsorption on CCHBi-3 (Li et al., 2013). In Fig. 6c, the high-resolution XPS of the O 1 s peak was deconvoluted into three component peaks at 530.18, 531.01, and 534.98 eV, attributed to BiO, −OH, and CO, respectively (Yang, Wan, et al., 2023). The relative proportion of CO increased from 12.8 % to 24.0 % after adsorption, suggesting π-π interaction in lignin adsorption. In the case of N 1 s (Fig. 6d), three component peaks with binding energies of 399.07, 399.55 and 401.49 eV were identified, attributed to –N=, –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively. After adsorption, the peaks at 399.55 and 401.49 eV shifted to higher binding energies of 400.02 and 404.24 eV, respectively, possibly due to hydrogen bonding between amino groups of CCHBi-3 and lignin, as well as electrostatic interaction between protonated amino groups of CCHBi-3 and lignin (Yang, Xu, et al., 2023). The high-resolution Bi 4 f spectra of CCHBi-3 in Fig. 6e are fitted to two peaks: Bi 4 f7/2 at 158.91 eV and Bi 4 f5/2 at 164.16 eV (Shao et al., 2019). The decrease in binding energies of these peaks after adsorption may be attributed to the protonation of BiP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of photocatalytic degradation of lignin
ce:section.ce:para: The light absorption properties of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 were examined using UV–vis DRS (Fig. 7a). Notably, CCHBi-3 exhibited the highest absorbance, attributed to the synergistic effect of the CDs' light-harvesting capability and the 3D porous structure. The band gap (Eg) for each sample was determined using the Kubelka-Munk function (Cirena et al., 2023), yielding values of 2.30 eV for BiP, 2.21 eV for CHBi-3, and 2.03 eV for CCHBi-3. The band structures of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 were investigated using valance band (VB)–XPS, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. The EVB values of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 were 1.79 eV, 1.67 eV, and 1.45 eV, respectively. Additionally, employing the formula Eg = EVB–ECB, the Econduction band (CB) values for BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 were calculated as −0.51 eV, −0.54 eV, and − 0.58 eV, respectively.

Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) experiments were conducted on BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 (Fig. 7c). An increasing trend was observed in the average fluorescence lifetimes of BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3, with values of 1.03 ns, 1.88 ns, and 3.11 ns, respectively. Fig. 7d illustrates the photoluminescence (PL) spectra excited at 435 nm, showing that CCHBi-3 exhibited the weakest PL peak intensity. CCHBi-3 exhibited a longer fluorescence lifetime and lower PL peak intensity, confirming that the incorporation of CDs@CNs significantly reduces the carrier recombination rate (Hu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023).

Fig. 7e presents the photocurrent response curves for BiP, CHBi-3, and CCHBi-3 under intermittent irradiation. CCHBi-3 exhibited a superior photocurrent response compared to BiP and CHBi-3. Additionally, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data (Fig. 7b) showed that CCHBi-3 had the smallest arc radius. This indicated that the presence of electronic conductivity paths between BiP and CDs@CNs enhanced carrier transport (Guo et al., 2024), thereby improving the photocatalytic performance of CCHBi-3. In conclusion, based on the preceding analyses, CCHBi-3 demonstrated superior photoelectric performance.

Trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis were conducted to identify the reactive species involved in lignin removal. Three scavengers—Na2-EDTA, benzoquinone (BQ), and methanol (CH3OH)—were used to trap active species: holes (h+), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and electrons (e−), respectively. Fig. 8a shows that the introduction of BQ and Na2-EDTA reduced the lignin removal rate of CCHBi-3 by 4.7 % and 51.0 %, respectively. Similarly, the lignin degradation rate decreased by 19.2 % with methanol addition. For reactions involving BQ, CH3OH, Na2-EDTA, and no scavenger (Fig. 8b), the corresponding k were 0.0112 ± 0.0020, 0.0066 ± 0.0016, 0.0029 ± 0.0002, and 0.0140 ± 0.0024 min−1, respectively. Additionally, EPR analysis was used to detect h+, ·O2 −, and ·OH, as illustrated in Fig. 8c-e. These results confirmed that h+, ·O2 −, and ·OH were the reactive species involved in lignin removal (Xu et al., 2020). Additionally, the potential degradation pathway of lignosulfonate was inferred from gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) measurements. The final products of lignosulfonate photocatalytic degradation, shown in Figs. S11 and S12, included small molecules such as H2O, CO2, and CO3 2−.

Fig. S13 illustrates the photocatalytic mechanism for lignin degradation using CCHBi. CDs@CNs and BiP were the primary components of CCHBi. As mentioned, the ECB and EVB of BiP were − 0.51 eV and 1.79 eV, respectively. The ECB and EVB of CDs@CNs were − 0.31 eV and 2.70 eV, respectively (Fig. S14). A Z-scheme mechanism was proposed. Under UV light irradiation, CDs@CNs and BiP were excited, generating electrons in the CB and holes in the VB, respectively. Due to the Z-scheme structure, photogenerated electrons in the CB of CDs@CNs combined with photogenerated holes in the VB of BiP, leaving residual photogenerated electrons in BiP and holes in CDs@CNs. The retained photogenerated electrons then combined with O₂ to generate ·O2 −, which further reduced lignin. Additionally, some holes in the VB of CDs@CNs oxidized OH− to ·OH, contributing to the oxidative degradation of lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of CCHBi-enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of lignin
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the preceding analysis, a mechanism for CCHBi-enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of lignin was proposed (Fig. 9 ). Initially, CCHBi had a 3D porous structure with surfaces rich in amino and hydroxyl groups, which enhanced lignin adsorption through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions. The introduction of CDs@CNs increased both the surface area and the number of amino and hydroxyl groups, further improving lignin adsorption (Chen et al., 2021). Upon UV light exposure, CDs@CNs and BiP were excited, generating electrons and holes. Subsequently, photogenerated electrons in the CB of CDs@CNs combined with photogenerated holes in the VB of BiP, leaving many photogenerated electrons in BiP and holes in CDs@CNs(Devi & Kavitha, 2013). Some of the retained electrons in BiP were converted into superoxide radicals, while some of the retained holes in CDs@CNs formed hydroxyl radicals. These photogenerated holes, superoxide radicals, and hydroxyl radicals effectively degraded lignin (Wu et al., 2023). The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation mechanisms could be described using the following formulas:

A novel photocatalytic adsorbent, CCHBi, was synthesized by incorporating BiP and CDs into a CNs hydrogel, which enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of lignin. The CNs-based hydrogel conferred several advantages to CCHBi: a larger adsorption capacity for lignin (435.0 ± 9.0 mg/g, 8.9 times greater than that of activated carbon), an improved apparent rate constant (0.0140 ± 0.0024 min−1, 3.1 times higher than BiP), and high stability (85 % of the initial removal rate retained after 10 cycles). Additionally, adsorption experiments and photoelectric performance demonstrated that CDs@CNs provided abundant adsorption sites for lignin, facilitated the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes, and improved stability. These findings pave the way for using CNs in developing new types of photocatalytic adsorbents.
